NCU TGI Drought Hub

Taiwan and Global Drought Investigation and Research Center

About Us

Taiwan and Global Drought Investigation and Research Center (TGI / DroughtHub) at National Central University is dedicated to integrating interdisciplinary research in climate science, hydrology, and geographic information technology. Our goal is to deepen the understanding of the causes and impacts of droughts in Taiwan and globally, promote sustainable water resource management, and assist society and industries in enhancing their resilience to climate change.

Our center brings together numerous experts and scholars and is equipped with advanced observation and analysis systems. We not only emphasize basic scientific research but also actively promote international cooperation and policy application, transforming research outcomes into concrete action plans to contribute value to society and global sustainable development.

Latest News

  1. 2025-09-28

    Call for Papers: International Special Issue Edited by Prof. Yuei-An Liou

    Prof. Yuei-An Liou from our research center is currently editing a special issue for the international journals Earth’s Future and GeoHealth on 'Impacts of Extreme Heat, Heatwaves, and Urban Heat Islands on Public Health: Vulnerability, Resilience, and Adaptation Strategies.' The call for papers is open until June 30, 2026. Researchers in related fields are cordially invited to submit...

    Read More →

  2. 2025-09-30

    RSETI Index Released by the Center

    Professor Yuei-An Liou’s research team has proposed the Relative Surface Evapotranspiration Index (RSETI), overcoming the limitations of existing remote sensing drought monitoring methods. The index successfully depicts the evolution of droughts in Australia from 2001 to 2022 and demonstrates its potential applications in agricultural management and climate adaptation. The results have been published in *Remote Sensing of Environment*.

    Read More →

  3. 2026-07-07

    [Call for Papers] ICEO&SI 2026 & CESR 2026 Special Issue: Earth's Future — Extreme Heat, Heat Waves, and Urban Heat Islands – Impacts on Public Health

    This special issue focuses on the vulnerability, resilience, and adaptation strategies related to public health impacts of extreme heat, heat waves, and urban heat islands. Qualified presentations at ICEO&SI 2026 and CESR 2026 are encouraged to submit enhanced versions of their work, and submissions from the broader research community are also warmly welcomed. Submission deadline: September 30, 2026.

    Read More →

  4. 2026-07-07

    [Call for Papers] ICEO&SI 2026 & CESR 2026 Special Issue: GeoHealth — Extreme Heat, Heat Waves, and Urban Heat Islands – Impacts on Public Health

    A coordinated companion special issue to the Earth's Future call, focusing on vulnerable populations, health risks, urban green infrastructure, and climate adaptation strategies, encouraging interdisciplinary research to advance global resilience and equity. Submission deadline: September 30, 2026.

    Read More →

  5. 2026-07-07

    [Call for Papers] ICEO&SI 2026 & CESR 2026 Special Issue: Remote Sensing — Remote Sensing of Extreme Heat Events, Urban Heat Islands, and Public Health Impacts

    This special issue integrates remote sensing, hydrological and atmospheric dynamics to address extreme heat events, urban heat islands, and their public health impacts. Qualified presentations at ICEO&SI 2026 and CESR 2026 are encouraged to submit enhanced versions of their work, and submissions integrating simulation analysis and adaptation strategies are warmly welcomed.

    Read More →

  6. 2025-11-24

    NCU and Australia's TNQ Drought Hub Hold Joint Workshop to Build Global Climate Resilience

    During the inaugural CESR forum, NCU hosted a joint workshop with the visiting TNQ Drought Hub delegation from Australia. By integrating NCU's satellite remote sensing technology with Australia's on-ground expertise, the two parties exchanged critical insights on drought monitoring and agricultural adaptation, laying a solid foundation for future transnational research collaboration.

    Read More →

  7. 2025-12-05

    Center Delegation Attends EME2025 in Vietnam, Deepening Transnational Green Sustainability Cooperation

    A delegation from the Center attended the 'EME2025 International Conference' hosted by VAST in Hanoi. Focusing on green development in Earth sciences, members exchanged insights on low-carbon strategies and digital transformation, actively expanding research networks in Southeast Asia to jointly address climate challenges.

    Read More →

  8. 2025-10-22

    [Research Achievement] RSETI: A New Drought Monitoring Index — Revealing 20 Years of Drought Spatiotemporal Trends in Australia

    To more accurately track drought changes, Professor Liu Shuo-An, Chair of the Space and Remote Sensing Research Center at National Central University, led a team to develop a novel drought monitoring index — the Relative Surface Evapotranspiration Index (RSETI). The results were published in *Remote Sensing of Environment* and effectively address the common interference from outliers, improving the efficiency of drought monitoring...

    Read More →

  9. 2025-10-29

    [Call for Papers] The Impacts of Extreme Heat, Heatwaves, and Urban Heat Islands on Public Health: Vulnerability, Resilience, and Adaptation Strategies

    This special issue addresses public health risks from extreme heat, heatwaves, and urban heat islands, focusing on vulnerable populations, health impacts, green infrastructure, and climate adaptation. Submission deadline: September 30, 2026, to *Earth’s Future* or *GeoHealth*.

    Read More →

  10. 2025-09-27

    CESR 2025 Open for Registration and Submissions

    The official website for the first 'International Forum on Climate Extremes and Societal Resilience (CESR 2025)' is now open for registration and abstract submissions. The forum will be held from November 24-25 (Mon-Tue), 2025, at National Central University, focusing on drought, heat stress, water resources...

    Read More →

  12. 2025-09-30

    RSETI Index Released by the Center

    Professor Yuei-An Liou’s research team has proposed the Relative Surface Evapotranspiration Index (RSETI), overcoming the limitations of existing remote sensing drought monitoring methods. The index successfully depicts the evolution of droughts in Australia from 2001 to 2022 and demonstrates its potential applications in agricultural management and climate adaptation. The results have been published in *Remote Sensing of Environment*.
